[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/%E5%A1%94%E5%AD%90%E6%B9%96%E4%BD%93%E8%82%B2%E4%B8%AD%E5%BF%83%E6%94%B9%E9%80%A0%E7%9A%84%E6%96%B9%E8%88%B1%E5%8C%BB%E9%99%A2_07.jpg/250px-%E5%A1%94%E5%AD%90%E6%B9%96%E4%BD%93%E8%82%B2%E4%B8%AD%E5%BF%83%E6%94%B9%E9%80%A0%E7%9A%84%E6%96%B9%E8%88%B1%E5%8C%BB%E9%99%A2_07.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E5%A1%94%E5%AD%90%E6%B9%96%E4%BD%93%E8%82%B2%E4%B8%AD%E5%BF%83%E6%94%B9%E9%80%A0%E7%9A%84%E6%96%B9%E8%88%B1%E5%8C%BB%E9%99%A2_07.jpg)

A [temporary hospital](/wiki/Fangcang_Hospital "Fangcang Hospital") constructed in Wuhan in February 2020

China opted for containment, instituting strict lockdowns to eliminate viral spread.[[273]](#cite_note-:02-274)[[274]](#cite_note-275) The vaccines distributed in China included the [BIBP](/wiki/Sinopharm_BIBP_COVID-19_vaccine "Sinopharm BIBP COVID-19 vaccine"), [WIBP](/wiki/Sinopharm_WIBP_COVID-19_vaccine "Sinopharm WIBP COVID-19 vaccine"), and [CoronaVac](/wiki/CoronaVac "CoronaVac").[[275]](#cite_note-276) It was reported on 11 December 2021, that China had vaccinated 1.162 billion of its citizens, or 82.5% of the total population of the country against COVID-19.[[276]](#cite_note-277) China's large-scale adoption of [zero-COVID](/wiki/Zero-COVID "Zero-COVID") had largely contained the first waves of infections of the disease.[[273]](#cite_note-:02-274)[[277]](#cite_note-278)[[278]](#cite_note-279) When the waves of infections due to the [Omicron variant](/wiki/SARS-CoV-2_Omicron_variant "SARS-CoV-2 Omicron variant") followed, China was almost alone in pursuing the strategy of zero-Covid to combat the spread of the virus in 2022.[[279]](#cite_note-280) Lockdown continued to be employed in November to combat a new wave of cases;[[280]](#cite_note-281)[[281]](#cite_note-282) however, [protests erupted in cities across China](/wiki/2022_COVID-19_protests_in_China "2022 COVID-19 protests in China") over the country's stringent measures,[[282]](#cite_note-283)[[283]](#cite_note-284) and in December that year, the country relaxed its zero-COVID policy.[[284]](#cite_note-285) On 20 December 2022, the Chinese State Council narrowed its definition of what would be counted as a COVID-19 death to include solely respiratory failure, which led to scepticism by health experts of the government's total death count[[285]](#cite_note-286)[[286]](#cite_note-287) at a time when hospitals reported being overwhelmed with cases following the abrupt discontinuation of zero-COVID.[[287]](#cite_note-288)

The first case in India was reported on 30 January 2020. India ordered a nationwide lockdown starting 24 March 2020,[[288]](#cite_note-mrGsK-289) with a phased unlock beginning 1 June 2020. Six cities accounted for around half of reported cases—[Mumbai](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Maharashtra "COVID-19 pandemic in Maharashtra"), [Delhi](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Delhi "COVID-19 pandemic in Delhi"), [Ahmedabad](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Gujarat "COVID-19 pandemic in Gujarat"), [Chennai](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Tamil_Nadu "COVID-19 pandemic in Tamil Nadu"), [Pune](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Maharashtra "COVID-19 pandemic in Maharashtra") and [Kolkata](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_West_Bengal "COVID-19 pandemic in West Bengal").[[289]](#cite_note-sHMHJ-290) Post-lockdown, the Government of India introduced a contact tracking app called [Aarogya Setu](/wiki/Aarogya_Setu "Aarogya Setu") to help authorities manage contact tracing and vaccine distribution.[[290]](#cite_note-291) India's vaccination programme was considered to be the world's largest and most successful with over 90% of citizens getting the first dose and another 65% getting the second dose.[[291]](#cite_note-292)[[292]](#cite_note-293) A second wave hit India in April 2021, straining healthcare services.[[293]](#cite_note-294) On 21 October 2021, it was reported that the country had surpassed 1 billion vaccinations.[[294]](#cite_note-295)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Disinfection_of_Tehran_subway_wagons_against_coronavirus_2020-02-26_09.jpg/250px-Disinfection_of_Tehran_subway_wagons_against_coronavirus_2020-02-26_09.jpg)](/wiki/File:Disinfection_of_Tehran_subway_wagons_against_coronavirus_2020-02-26_09.jpg)

Disinfection of [Tehran Metro](/wiki/Tehran_Metro "Tehran Metro") trains to prevent COVID-19 transmission. Similar measures have also been taken in other countries.[[295]](#cite_note-2kQpo-296)

Iran reported its first confirmed cases on 19 February 2020, in [Qom](/wiki/Qom "Qom").[[296]](#cite_note-wHBRB-297)[[297]](#cite_note-4yrqM-298) Early measures included the cancellation/closure of concerts and other cultural events,[[298]](#cite_note-Xm5nq-299) Friday prayers,[[299]](#cite_note-Urdn6-300) and school and university campuses.[[300]](#cite_note-2YX6a-301) Iran became a centre of the pandemic in February 2020.[[301]](#cite_note-HHOnl-302)[[302]](#cite_note-New_Yorker-303) More than ten countries had traced their outbreaks to Iran by 28 February, indicating a more severe outbreak than the 388 reported cases.[[302]](#cite_note-New_Yorker-303)[[303]](#cite_note-D2Xpc-304) The [Iranian Parliament](/wiki/Islamic_Consultative_Assembly "Islamic Consultative Assembly") closed, after 23 of its 290 members tested positive on 3 March 2020.[[304]](#cite_note-fXv2K-305) At least twelve sitting or former Iranian politicians and government officials had died by 17 March 2020.[[305]](#cite_note-oxtUC-306) By August 2021, the pandemic's fifth wave peaked, with more than 400 deaths in 1 day.[[306]](#cite_note-307)

COVID-19 was confirmed in South Korea on 20 January 2020. Military bases were quarantined after tests showed three infected soldiers.[[307]](#cite_note-b82186-308) South Korea introduced what was then considered the world's largest and best-organised screening programme, isolating infected people, and tracing and quarantining contacts.[[308]](#cite_note-science20200317-309) Screening methods included mandatory self-reporting by new international arrivals through mobile application,[[309]](#cite_note-nbcnews1167376-310) combined with [drive-through](/wiki/Drive-through "Drive-through") testing,[[310]](#cite_note-CvyZS-311) and increasing testing capability to 20,000 people/day.[[311]](#cite_note-fdhQW-312) Despite some early criticisms,[[312]](#cite_note-imx38-313) South Korea's programme was considered a success in controlling the outbreak without quarantining entire cities.[[308]](#cite_note-science20200317-309)[[313]](#cite_note-Wypis-314)[[314]](#cite_note-joins23778577-315)

### Europe

Main article: [COVID-19 pandemic in Europe](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Europe "COVID-19 pandemic in Europe")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Persons_died_due_to_coronavirus_COVID-19_per_capita_in_Europe.svg/500px-Persons_died_due_to_coronavirus_COVID-19_per_capita_in_Europe.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Persons_died_due_to_coronavirus_COVID-19_per_capita_in_Europe.svg)

Deaths per 100,000 residents

The COVID-19 pandemic arrived in Europe with its first confirmed case in [Bordeaux](/wiki/Bordeaux "Bordeaux"), [France](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_France "COVID-19 pandemic in France"), on 24 January 2020, and subsequently spread widely across the continent. By 17 March 2020, every country in Europe had confirmed a case,[[315]](#cite_note-316) and all had reported at least one death, with the exception of [Vatican City](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Vatican_City "COVID-19 pandemic in Vatican City"). Italy was the first European nation to experience a major outbreak in early 2020, becoming the first country worldwide to introduce a national [lockdown](/wiki/COVID-19_lockdowns_in_Italy "COVID-19 lockdowns in Italy").[[316]](#cite_note-317) By 13 March 2020, the WHO declared Europe the epicentre of the pandemic[[317]](#cite_note-318)[[318]](#cite_note-319) and it remained so until the WHO announced it had been overtaken by [South America](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_South_America "COVID-19 pandemic in South America") on 22 May.[[319]](#cite_note-320) By 18 March 2020, more than 250 million people were in [lockdown](/wiki/Stay-at-home_order "Stay-at-home order") in Europe.[[320]](#cite_note-321) Despite [deployment of COVID-19 vaccines](/wiki/Deployment_of_COVID-19_vaccines "Deployment of COVID-19 vaccines"), Europe became the pandemic's epicentre once again in late 2021.[[321]](#cite_note-322)[[322]](#cite_note-323)

The [Italian outbreak](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Italy "COVID-19 pandemic in Italy") began on 31 January 2020, when two Chinese tourists tested positive for SARS-CoV-2 in Rome.[[213]](#cite_note-Corriere_20Jan-214) Cases began to rise sharply, which prompted the government to suspend flights to and from China and declare a state of emergency.[[323]](#cite_note-thelocal-flight-324) On 22 February 2020, the Council of Ministers announced a new decree-law to contain the outbreak, which quarantined more than 50,000 people in northern Italy.[[324]](#cite_note-AutoDW-201-325) On 4 March, the Italian government ordered schools and universities closed as Italy reached a hundred deaths. Sport was suspended completely for at least one month.[[325]](#cite_note-Kv4Ld-326) On 11 March, Italian Prime Minister Giuseppe Conte closed down nearly all commercial activity except supermarkets and pharmacies.[[326]](#cite_note-To26Y-327)[[327]](#cite_note-ClzT6-328) On 19 April, the first wave ebbed, as 7-day deaths declined to 433.[[328]](#cite_note-thehill493586-329) On 13 October, the Italian government again issued restrictive rules to contain the second wave.[[329]](#cite_note-KhaW7-330) On 10 November, Italy surpassed 1 million confirmed infections.[[330]](#cite_note-8XEwk-331) On 23 November, it was reported that the second wave of the virus had led some hospitals to stop accepting patients.[[331]](#cite_note-332)

[![Elderly woman rolls up sleeve as two nurses administer a vaccine.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/2020_12_27_mas_vacunados_en_la_residencia_mixta_1_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-2020_12_27_mas_vacunados_en_la_residencia_mixta_1_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:2020_12_27_mas_vacunados_en_la_residencia_mixta_1_(cropped).jpg)

Vaccinations at a [retirement home](/wiki/Retirement_home "Retirement home") in [Gijón](/wiki/Gij%C3%B3n "Gijón"), Spain on 12 December 2020

The virus was first confirmed to have spread to Spain on 31 January 2020, when a German tourist tested positive for SARS-CoV-2 on [La Gomera](/wiki/La_Gomera "La Gomera") in the Canary Islands.[[332]](#cite_note-SanidadConfirmaEnLaGomera-333) Post-hoc genetic analysis has shown that at least 15 strains of the virus had been imported, and [community transmission](/wiki/Community_transmission "Community transmission") began by mid-February.[[333]](#cite_note-genetic_analysis-334) On 29 March, it was announced that, beginning the following day, all non-essential workers were ordered to remain at home for the next 14 days.[[334]](#cite_note-335) The number of cases increased again in July in a number of cities including [Barcelona](/wiki/Barcelona "Barcelona"), [Zaragoza](/wiki/Zaragoza "Zaragoza") and [Madrid](/wiki/Madrid "Madrid"), which led to reimposition of some restrictions but no national lockdown.[[335]](#cite_note-bbc25july-336)[[336]](#cite_note-elpais6aug-337)[[337]](#cite_note-telegraph_aug16-338)[[338]](#cite_note-339) By September 2021, Spain was one of the countries with the highest percentage of its population vaccinated (76% fully vaccinated and 79% with the first dose).[[339]](#cite_note-auto2-340) Italy is ranked second at 75%.[[339]](#cite_note-auto2-340)

Sweden differed from most other European countries in that it mostly remained open.[[340]](#cite_note-NYT20200515-341) Per the [Swedish constitution](/wiki/Swedish_constitution "Swedish constitution"), the [Public Health Agency of Sweden](/wiki/Public_Health_Agency_of_Sweden "Public Health Agency of Sweden") has autonomy that prevents political interference and the agency favoured remaining open. The Swedish strategy focused on longer-term measures, based on the assumption that after lockdown the virus would resume spreading, with the same result.[[341]](#cite_note-7ys2k-342)[[342]](#cite_note-I06mN-343) By the end of June, Sweden no longer had [excess mortality](/wiki/Excess_mortality "Excess mortality").[[343]](#cite_note-HEUrz-344)

[Devolution in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Devolution_in_the_United_Kingdom "Devolution in the United Kingdom") meant that each of its four [countries](/wiki/Countries_of_the_United_Kingdom "Countries of the United Kingdom") developed its own response. England's restrictions were shorter-lived than the others.[[344]](#cite_note-8VnMf-345) The [UK government](/wiki/Government_of_the_United_Kingdom "Government of the United Kingdom") started enforcing social distancing and quarantine measures on 18 March 2020.[[345]](#cite_note-PkWGD-346)[[346]](#cite_note-Pkx25-347) On 16 March, Prime Minister [Boris Johnson](/wiki/Boris_Johnson "Boris Johnson") advised against non-essential travel and social contact, praising [work from home](/wiki/Remote_work "Remote work") and avoiding venues such as pubs, restaurants, and theatres.[[347]](#cite_note-5Q93q-348)[[348]](#cite_note-hYdUy-349) On 20 March, the government ordered all leisure establishments to close,[[349]](#cite_note-dmBNt-350) and promised to prevent unemployment.[[350]](#cite_note-mV2p3-351) On 23 March, Johnson banned gatherings and restricted non-essential travel and outdoor activity. Unlike previous measures, these restrictions were enforceable by police through fines and dispersal of gatherings. Most non-essential businesses were ordered to close.[[351]](#cite_note-bbc-pm-2403-352) On 24 April 2020, it was reported that a promising vaccine trial had begun in England; the government pledged more than £50 million towards research.[[352]](#cite_note-vKeXC-353) On 16 April 2020, it was reported that the UK would have first access to the Oxford vaccine, due to a prior contract; should the trial be successful, some 30 million doses would be available.[[353]](#cite_note-Lbt4o-354) On 2 December 2020, the UK became the first developed country to approve the Pfizer vaccine; 800,000 doses were immediately available for use.[[354]](#cite_note-355) In August 2022 it was reported that viral infection cases had declined in the UK.[[355]](#cite_note-356)

### North America

Main article: [COVID-19 pandemic in North America](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_North_America "COVID-19 pandemic in North America")

The virus arrived in the [United States](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_States "COVID-19 pandemic in the United States") on 13 January 2020.[[356]](#cite_note-357) Cases were reported in all North American countries after [Saint Kitts and Nevis](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Saint_Kitts_and_Nevis "COVID-19 pandemic in Saint Kitts and Nevis") confirmed a case on 25 March, and in all North American territories after [Bonaire](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Bonaire "COVID-19 pandemic in Bonaire") confirmed a case on 16 April.[[357]](#cite_note-FirstBonaire_local-358)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/USNS_Comfort_New_York_City_2020.jpg/250px-USNS_Comfort_New_York_City_2020.jpg)](/wiki/File:USNS_Comfort_New_York_City_2020.jpg)

The hospital ship [USNS *Comfort*](/wiki/USNS_Comfort "USNS Comfort") arrives in [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") on 30 March 2020.

Per *Our World in Data*, 103,436,829[[5]](#cite_note-Template:COVID-19_data-5) confirmed cases have been reported in the United States with 1,235,885[[5]](#cite_note-Template:COVID-19_data-5) deaths, the most of any country, and [the nineteenth-highest](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_death_rates_by_country "COVID-19 pandemic death rates by country") per capita worldwide.[[358]](#cite_note-JH_Mortality-359) COVID-19 is the [deadliest pandemic in US history](/wiki/List_of_disasters_in_the_United_States_by_death_toll "List of disasters in the United States by death toll");[[359]](#cite_note-360) it was the third-leading cause of death in the US in 2020, behind heart disease and cancer.[[360]](#cite_note-361) From 2019 to 2020, US life expectancy dropped by 3 years for Hispanic Americans, 2.9 years for African Americans, and 1.2 years for white Americans.[[361]](#cite_note-Bosman-362) These effects have persisted as US deaths due to COVID-19 in 2021 exceeded those in 2020.[[362]](#cite_note-363) In the United States, COVID-19 vaccines became available under emergency use in December 2020, beginning the [national vaccination programme](/wiki/COVID-19_vaccination_in_the_United_States "COVID-19 vaccination in the United States"). The first COVID-19 vaccine was officially approved by the [Food and Drug Administration](/wiki/Food_and_Drug_Administration "Food and Drug Administration") on 23 August 2021.[[363]](#cite_note-364) By 18 November 2022, while cases in the U.S. had declined, COVID variants BQ.1/BQ.1.1 had become dominant in the country.[[364]](#cite_note-365)[[365]](#cite_note-366)

In March 2020, as cases of community transmission were confirmed across [Canada](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Canada "COVID-19 pandemic in Canada"), all of its provinces and territories declared states of emergency. Provinces and territories, to varying degrees, implemented school and daycare closures, prohibitions on gatherings, closures of non-essential businesses and restrictions on entry. Canada severely restricted its border access, barring travellers from all countries with some exceptions.[[366]](#cite_note-367) Cases surged across Canada, notably in the provinces of [British Columbia](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_British_Columbia "COVID-19 pandemic in British Columbia"), [Alberta](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Alberta "COVID-19 pandemic in Alberta"), [Quebec](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Quebec "COVID-19 pandemic in Quebec") and [Ontario](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Ontario "COVID-19 pandemic in Ontario"), with the formation of the [Atlantic Bubble](/wiki/Atlantic_Bubble "Atlantic Bubble"), a [travel-restricted](/wiki/Travel_restrictions_related_to_the_COVID-19_pandemic "Travel restrictions related to the COVID-19 pandemic") area of the country (formed of the four [Atlantic provinces](/wiki/Atlantic_Canada "Atlantic Canada")).[[367]](#cite_note-368) Vaccine passports were adopted in all provinces and two of the territories.[[368]](#cite_note-369)[[369]](#cite_note-370) Per a report on 11 November 2022, Canadian health authorities saw a surge in influenza, while COVID-19 was expected to rise during winter.[[370]](#cite_note-371)

### South America

Main article: [COVID-19 pandemic in South America](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_South_America "COVID-19 pandemic in South America")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Fosas_comunes_en_Cochabamba.jpg/250px-Fosas_comunes_en_Cochabamba.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fosas_comunes_en_Cochabamba.jpg)

Mass graves being prepared for COVID-19 victims in [Cochabamba](/wiki/Cochabamba "Cochabamba"), Bolivia

The COVID-19 pandemic was confirmed to have reached South America on 26 February 2020, when Brazil confirmed a case in [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo "São Paulo").[[371]](#cite_note-372) By 3 April, all countries and territories in South America had recorded at least one case.[[372]](#cite_note-Stuff/Fairfax-373) On 13 May 2020, it was reported that Latin America and the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean") had reported over 400,000 cases of COVID-19 infection with 23,091 deaths. On 22 May 2020, citing the rapid increase of [infections in Brazil](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Brazil "COVID-19 pandemic in Brazil"), the WHO declared South America the epicentre of the pandemic.[[373]](#cite_note-374)[[374]](#cite_note-375) As of 16 July 2021, South America had recorded 34,359,631 confirmed cases and 1,047,229 deaths from COVID-19. Due to a shortage of testing and medical facilities, it is believed that the outbreak is far larger than the official numbers show.[[375]](#cite_note-376)

The virus was confirmed to have spread to Brazil on 25 February 2020,[[376]](#cite_note-377) when a man from [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo "São Paulo") who had travelled to Italy tested positive for the virus.[[377]](#cite_note-378) The disease had spread to every [federative unit of Brazil](/wiki/Federative_units_of_Brazil "Federative units of Brazil") by 21 March. On 19 June 2020, the country reported its one millionth case and nearly 49,000 reported deaths.[[378]](#cite_note-379)[[379]](#cite_note-380) One estimate of [under-reporting](/wiki/Under-reporting#Disease "Under-reporting") was 22.62% of total reported COVID-19 mortality in 2020.[[380]](#cite_note-381)[[381]](#cite_note-382)[[382]](#cite_note-383) As of 12 March 2026, Brazil, with 37,978,000[[5]](#cite_note-Template:COVID-19_data-5) confirmed cases and 703,774[[5]](#cite_note-Template:COVID-19_data-5) deaths, has the third-highest number of confirmed cases and second-highest death toll from COVID-19 in the world, behind only [those of the United States](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_States "COVID-19 pandemic in the United States") and [India](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_India "COVID-19 pandemic in India").[[383]](#cite_note-384)

### Africa

Main article: [COVID-19 pandemic in Africa](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Africa "COVID-19 pandemic in Africa")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Air_Forces_Africa_delivers_medical_supplies_to_Ghana_%2849829156488%29.jpg/250px-Air_Forces_Africa_delivers_medical_supplies_to_Ghana_%2849829156488%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Air_Forces_Africa_delivers_medical_supplies_to_Ghana_(49829156488).jpg)

[US Air Force](/wiki/United_States_Air_Force "United States Air Force") personnel unload a [C-17](/wiki/Boeing_C-17_Globemaster_III "Boeing C-17 Globemaster III") aircraft carrying medical supplies in [Niamey](/wiki/Niamey "Niamey"), Niger, in April 2020.

The COVID-19 pandemic was confirmed to have spread to Africa on 14 February 2020, with the first confirmed case announced in [Egypt](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Egypt "COVID-19 pandemic in Egypt").[[384]](#cite_note-bbc_51509248-385)[[385]](#cite_note-386) The first confirmed case in [sub-Saharan Africa](/wiki/Sub-Saharan_Africa "Sub-Saharan Africa") was announced in [Nigeria](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Nigeria "COVID-19 pandemic in Nigeria") at the end of February 2020.[[386]](#cite_note-bbc_51671834-387) Within three months, the virus had spread throughout the continent; [Lesotho](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Lesotho "COVID-19 pandemic in Lesotho"), the last African sovereign state to have remained free of the virus, reported its first case on 13 May 2020.[[387]](#cite_note-reuters_idUSKBN22P1R4-388)[[388]](#cite_note-latimes.com-389) By 26 May, it appeared that most African countries were experiencing community transmission, although testing capacity was limited.[[389]](#cite_note-theguardian_20200526_africa-390) Most of the identified imported cases arrived from Europe and the United States rather than from China where the virus originated.[[390]](#cite_note-Africa_Braces-391) Many preventive measures [were implemented](/wiki/National_responses_to_the_COVID-19_pandemic_in_Africa "National responses to the COVID-19 pandemic in Africa") by different countries in Africa including travel restrictions, flight cancellations, and event cancellations.[[391]](#cite_note-392) Despite fears, Africa reported lower death rates than other, more economically developed regions.[[392]](#cite_note-NYT_Africa_deaths-393)

In early June 2021, Africa faced a third wave of COVID infections with cases rising in 14 countries.[[393]](#cite_note-theguardian_20210607_third-394) By 4 July the continent recorded more than 251,000 new COVID cases, a 20% increase from the prior week and a 12% increase from the January peak. More than sixteen African countries, including [Malawi](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Malawi "COVID-19 pandemic in Malawi") and [Senegal](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Senegal "COVID-19 pandemic in Senegal"), recorded an uptick in new cases.[[394]](#cite_note-Mendez-395) The WHO labelled it Africa's 'Worst Pandemic Week Ever'.[[395]](#cite_note-396) In October 2022, WHO reported that most countries on the African continent will miss the goal of 70 per cent vaccination by the end of 2022.[[396]](#cite_note-397)

### Oceania

Main article: [COVID-19 pandemic in Oceania](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Oceania "COVID-19 pandemic in Oceania")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Empty_shelves_at_Coles_during_the_COVID-19_pandemic_in_Brisbane%2C_Australia.jpg/250px-Empty_shelves_at_Coles_during_the_COVID-19_pandemic_in_Brisbane%2C_Australia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Empty_shelves_at_Coles_during_the_COVID-19_pandemic_in_Brisbane,_Australia.jpg)

Empty shelves at a [Coles](/wiki/Coles_Supermarkets "Coles Supermarkets") grocery store in [Brisbane](/wiki/Brisbane "Brisbane"), Australia, in April 2020

The COVID-19 pandemic was confirmed to have reached Oceania on 25 January 2020, with the first confirmed case reported in [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne "Melbourne"), [Australia](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Australia "COVID-19 pandemic in Australia").[[397]](#cite_note-AustraliaCase1-398)[[398]](#cite_note-Brisbane2021-399) It has since spread elsewhere in the region.[[399]](#cite_note-WHO_Dashboard-400)[[398]](#cite_note-Brisbane2021-399) [Australia](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Australia "COVID-19 pandemic in Australia") and [New Zealand](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_New_Zealand "COVID-19 pandemic in New Zealand") were praised for their handling of the pandemic in comparison to other Western nations, with New Zealand and each state in Australia wiping out all community transmission of the virus several times even after re-introduction into the community.[[400]](#cite_note-reuters-oz-10days-401)[[401]](#cite_note-bloomberg-oz-tassie-price-402)[[402]](#cite_note-403)

As a result of the high transmissibility of the Delta variant, however, by August 2021, the Australian states of [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales") and [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_(Australia) "Victoria (Australia)") had conceded defeat in their eradication efforts.[[403]](#cite_note-404) In early October 2021, New Zealand also abandoned its elimination strategy.[[404]](#cite_note-The_Guardian_NZ_abandons_elimination-405) In November and December, following vaccination efforts, the remaining states of Australia, excluding Western Australia, voluntarily gave up [COVID-Zero](/wiki/Zero-COVID "Zero-COVID") to open up state and international borders.[[405]](#cite_note-406)[[406]](#cite_note-407)[[407]](#cite_note-408) The open borders allowed the Omicron variant of COVID-19 to enter quickly, and cases subsequently exceeded 120,000 a day.[[408]](#cite_note-409) By early March 2022, with cases exceeding 1,000 a day, Western Australia conceded defeat in its eradication strategy and opened its borders.[[409]](#cite_note-410) Despite record cases, Australian jurisdictions slowly removed restrictions such as close contact isolation, mask wearing, and density limits by April 2022.[[410]](#cite_note-411)

On 9 September 2022, restrictions were significantly relaxed. The aircraft mask mandate was scrapped nationwide, and daily reporting transitioned to weekly reporting.[[411]](#cite_note-412)[[412]](#cite_note-413)[[413]](#cite_note-414) On 14 September, COVID-19 disaster payment for isolating persons was extended for mandatory isolation.[[414]](#cite_note-415) By 22 September, all states had ended mask mandates on public transport, including in Victoria, where the mandate had lasted for approximately 800 days.[[415]](#cite_note-416) On 30 September 2022, all Australian leaders declared the emergency response finished and announced the end of isolation requirements. These changes were due in part to high levels of 'hybrid immunity' and low case numbers.[[416]](#cite_note-417)

### Antarctica

Main article: [COVID-19 pandemic in Antarctica](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Antarctica "COVID-19 pandemic in Antarctica")

Due to its remoteness and sparse population, [Antarctica](/wiki/Antarctica "Antarctica") was the last continent to have confirmed cases of COVID-19.[[417]](#cite_note-418)[[418]](#cite_note-419)[[419]](#cite_note-420) The first cases were reported in December 2020, almost a year after the first cases of COVID-19 were detected in China. At least 36 people were infected in the first outbreak in 2020,[[420]](#cite_note-421) with several other outbreaks taking place in 2021 and 2022.[[421]](#cite_note-422)

### United Nations

Main article: [United Nations response to the COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/United_Nations_response_to_the_COVID-19_pandemic "United Nations response to the COVID-19 pandemic")

The [United Nations Conference on Trade and Development](/wiki/United_Nations_Conference_on_Trade_and_Development "United Nations Conference on Trade and Development") (UNSC) was criticised for its slow response, especially regarding the UN's [global ceasefire](/wiki/Global_ceasefire "Global ceasefire"), which aimed to open up humanitarian access to conflict zones.[[422]](#cite_note-:22-423)[[423]](#cite_note-Krm4a-424) The United Nations Security Council was criticised due to the inadequate manner in which it dealt with the COVID-19 pandemic, namely the poor ability to create international collaboration during this crisis.[[424]](#cite_note-425)[[425]](#cite_note-426)

On 23 March 2020, United Nations Secretary-General [António Manuel de Oliveira Guterres](/wiki/Ant%C3%B3nio_Guterres "António Guterres") appealed for a global [ceasefire](/wiki/Ceasefire "Ceasefire");[[426]](#cite_note-tFb4N-427)[[427]](#cite_note-lzoeV-428) 172 UN member states and observers signed a non-binding supporting statement in June,[[428]](#cite_note-AGhvz-429) and the [UN Security Council](/wiki/UN_Security_Council "UN Security Council") passed a [resolution](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council_Resolution_2532 "United Nations Security Council Resolution 2532") supporting it in July.[[429]](#cite_note-9rN79-430)[[430]](#cite_note-nJTuX-431)

On 29 September 2020, Guterres urged the [International Monetary Fund](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund") to help certain countries via debt relief and also call for countries to increase contributions to develop vaccines.[[431]](#cite_note-432)

### WHO

Main article: [World Health Organization's response to the COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/World_Health_Organization%27s_response_to_the_COVID-19_pandemic "World Health Organization's response to the COVID-19 pandemic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/13981214000744637189356214810969_%D9%86%D8%B4%D8%B3%D8%AA_%D9%85%D8%AF%DB%8C%D8%B1%D8%A7%D9%86_%D8%B4%D9%87%D8%B1%DB%8C_%D8%A8%D8%A7_%D9%86%D9%85%D8%A7%DB%8C%D9%86%D8%AF%DA%AF%D8%A7%D9%86_%D8%B3%D8%A7%D8%B2%D9%85%D8%A7%D9%86_%D8%A8%D9%87%D8%AF%D8%A7%D8%B4%D8%AA_%D8%AC%D9%87%D8%A7%D9%86%DB%8C.jpg/250px-13981214000744637189356214810969_%D9%86%D8%B4%D8%B3%D8%AA_%D9%85%D8%AF%DB%8C%D8%B1%D8%A7%D9%86_%D8%B4%D9%87%D8%B1%DB%8C_%D8%A8%D8%A7_%D9%86%D9%85%D8%A7%DB%8C%D9%86%D8%AF%DA%AF%D8%A7%D9%86_%D8%B3%D8%A7%D8%B2%D9%85%D8%A7%D9%86_%D8%A8%D9%87%D8%AF%D8%A7%D8%B4%D8%AA_%D8%AC%D9%87%D8%A7%D9%86%DB%8C.jpg)](/wiki/File:13981214000744637189356214810969_%D9%86%D8%B4%D8%B3%D8%AA_%D9%85%D8%AF%DB%8C%D8%B1%D8%A7%D9%86_%D8%B4%D9%87%D8%B1%DB%8C_%D8%A8%D8%A7_%D9%86%D9%85%D8%A7%DB%8C%D9%86%D8%AF%DA%AF%D8%A7%D9%86_%D8%B3%D8%A7%D8%B2%D9%85%D8%A7%D9%86_%D8%A8%D9%87%D8%AF%D8%A7%D8%B4%D8%AA_%D8%AC%D9%87%D8%A7%D9%86%DB%8C.jpg)

[World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization") representatives holding joint meeting with [Tehran](/wiki/Tehran "Tehran") city administrators in March 2020

The WHO spearheaded initiatives such as the [COVID-19 Solidarity Response Fund](/wiki/COVID-19_Solidarity_Response_Fund "COVID-19 Solidarity Response Fund") to raise money for the pandemic response, the [UN COVID-19 Supply Chain Task Force](/wiki/UN_COVID-19_Supply_Chain_Task_Force "UN COVID-19 Supply Chain Task Force"), and the [solidarity trial](/wiki/Solidarity_trial "Solidarity trial") for investigating potential treatment options for the disease. The [COVAX](/wiki/COVAX "COVAX") programme, co-led by the WHO, [GAVI](/wiki/GAVI "GAVI"), and the [Coalition for Epidemic Preparedness Innovations](/wiki/Coalition_for_Epidemic_Preparedness_Innovations "Coalition for Epidemic Preparedness Innovations") (CEPI), aimed to accelerate the development, manufacture, and distribution of COVID-19 vaccines, and to guarantee fair and equitable access across the world.[[432]](#cite_note-433)[[433]](#cite_note-434)

## Restrictions

Further information: [Timeline of the COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/Timeline_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic "Timeline of the COVID-19 pandemic") and [International aid related to the COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/International_aid_related_to_the_COVID-19_pandemic "International aid related to the COVID-19 pandemic")

[![Workers unloading boxes of medical supplies at Villamor Air Base.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/China_COVID19_test_kit_PH_donation_8.jpg/250px-China_COVID19_test_kit_PH_donation_8.jpg)](/wiki/File:China_COVID19_test_kit_PH_donation_8.jpg)

Donated medical supplies received in the Philippines

The pandemic shook the world's economy, with especially severe economic damage in the United States, Europe and Latin America.[[434]](#cite_note-435)[[435]](#cite_note-Effect_of_COVID-19_on_food_security-436) A consensus report by American intelligence agencies in April 2021 concluded, "Efforts to contain and manage the virus have reinforced nationalist trends globally, as some states turned inward to protect their citizens and sometimes cast blame on marginalised groups". COVID-19 inflamed partisanship and polarisation around the world as bitter arguments exploded over how to respond. International trade was disrupted amid the formation of no-entry enclaves.[[436]](#cite_note-437)

### Travel restrictions

Main article: [Travel restrictions related to the COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/Travel_restrictions_related_to_the_COVID-19_pandemic "Travel restrictions related to the COVID-19 pandemic")

The pandemic led many countries and regions to impose quarantines, entry bans, or other restrictions, either for citizens, recent travellers to affected areas,[[437]](#cite_note-20200326nytimes-438) or for all travellers.[[438]](#cite_note-439)[[439]](#cite_note-440) Travel collapsed worldwide, damaging the travel sector. The effectiveness of travel restrictions was questioned as the virus spread across the world.[[440]](#cite_note-20200224nationalgeographic-441) One study found that travel restrictions only modestly affected the initial spread, unless combined with other [infection prevention and control](/wiki/Infection_prevention_and_control "Infection prevention and control") measures.[[441]](#cite_note-DJYtB-442)[[442]](#cite_note-443) Researchers concluded that "travel restrictions are most useful in the early and late phase of an epidemic" and "restrictions of travel from Wuhan unfortunately came too late".[[443]](#cite_note-283GD-444) The European Union rejected the idea of suspending the [Schengen free travel zone](/wiki/Schengen_Agreement "Schengen Agreement").[[444]](#cite_note-dw52497811-445)[[445]](#cite_note-20200313euractiv-446)

### Repatriation of foreign citizens

Main article: [Evacuations related to the COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/Evacuations_related_to_the_COVID-19_pandemic "Evacuations related to the COVID-19 pandemic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Ukraine_evacuates_Ukrainian_and_foreign_citizens_from_Wuhan_16.jpg/250px-Ukraine_evacuates_Ukrainian_and_foreign_citizens_from_Wuhan_16.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ukraine_evacuates_Ukrainian_and_foreign_citizens_from_Wuhan_16.jpg)

Ukraine evacuates Ukrainian citizens from [Wuhan](/wiki/Wuhan "Wuhan"), China.

Several countries repatriated their citizens and diplomatic staff from Wuhan and surrounding areas, primarily through [charter flights](/wiki/Air_charter "Air charter"). Canada, the United States, Japan, India, Sri Lanka, Australia, France, Argentina, Germany and Thailand were among the first to do so.[[446]](#cite_note-AutoDW-169-447) Brazil and New Zealand evacuated their own nationals and others.[[447]](#cite_note-AutoDW-171-448)[[448]](#cite_note-6l9jG-449) On 14 March, South Africa repatriated 112 South Africans who tested negative, while four who showed symptoms were left behind.[[449]](#cite_note-auto3-450) Pakistan declined to evacuate its citizens.[[450]](#cite_note-20200131dialoguepakistan-451)

On 15 February, the US announced it would evacuate Americans aboard the *[Diamond Princess](/wiki/Diamond_Princess_outbreak "Diamond Princess outbreak")* cruise ship,[[451]](#cite_note-AutoDW-174-452) and on 21 February, Canada evacuated 129 Canadians from the ship.[[452]](#cite_note-AutoDW-175-453) In early March, the Indian government began repatriating its citizens from Iran.[[453]](#cite_note-czjOG-454)[[454]](#cite_note-indiatimes74647353-455) On 20 March, the United States began to withdraw some troops from Iraq.[[455]](#cite_note-USWithdraw-456)

## Impact

Main article: [Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/Impact_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic "Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic")

Further information: [Social impact of the COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/Social_impact_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic "Social impact of the COVID-19 pandemic")

### Economics

Main article: [Economic impact of the COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/Economic_impact_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic "Economic impact of the COVID-19 pandemic")

See also: [Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on aviation](/wiki/Impact_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic_on_aviation "Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on aviation"), [on science and technology](/wiki/Impact_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic_on_science_and_technology "Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on science and technology"), [on financial markets](/wiki/Financial_market_impact_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic "Financial market impact of the COVID-19 pandemic"), [2020 stock market crash](/wiki/2020_stock_market_crash "2020 stock market crash"), and [COVID-19 recession](/wiki/COVID-19_recession "COVID-19 recession")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Stock-indices-2020crash%2Brecovery.svg/250px-Stock-indices-2020crash%2Brecovery.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Stock-indices-2020crash%2Brecovery.svg)

A [stock index](/wiki/Stock_index "Stock index") chart shows the [2020 stock market crash](/wiki/2020_stock_market_crash "2020 stock market crash").

The pandemic and responses to it damaged the global economy. On 27 February 2020, worries about the outbreak crushed US stock indexes, which posted their sharpest falls since 2008.[[456]](#cite_note-U7teI-457)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/1990-_Cruise_ship_passenger_count_-_annually.svg/250px-1990-_Cruise_ship_passenger_count_-_annually.svg.png)](/wiki/File:1990-_Cruise_ship_passenger_count_-_annually.svg)

Industries such as [cruising](/wiki/Cruise_line "Cruise line") experienced a significant decline—down to levels not seen in thirty years.[[457]](#cite_note-Cruise_202412-458)

Tourism collapsed due to travel restrictions, closing of public places including travel attractions, and advice of governments against travel. Airlines cancelled flights, while British regional airline [Flybe](/wiki/Flybe_(1979%E2%80%932020) "Flybe (1979–2020)") collapsed.[[458]](#cite_note-z2VrM-459) The cruise line industry was hard hit,[[459]](#cite_note-smh20200227-460) and train stations and ferry ports closed.[[460]](#cite_note-wnn7L-461) International mail stopped or was delayed.[[461]](#cite_note-wsj1158868721-462)

The retail sector faced reductions in store hours or closures.[[462]](#cite_note-463) Retailers in Europe and Latin America faced traffic declines of 40 per cent. North America and Middle East retailers saw a 50–60 per cent drop.[[463]](#cite_note-20200402aislelabs-464) Shopping centres faced a 33–43 per cent drop in foot traffic in March compared to February. Mall operators around the world coped by increasing sanitation, installing thermal scanners to check the temperature of shoppers, and cancelling events.[[464]](#cite_note-J6Y6Z-465)

Hundreds of millions of jobs were lost,[[465]](#cite_note-aljazeera2004271718-466)[[466]](#cite_note-20200506csmonitor-467) including more than 40 million jobs in the US.[[467]](#cite_note-20200528WaPo-468) According to a report by [Yelp](/wiki/Yelp "Yelp"), about 60% of US businesses that closed will stay shut permanently.[[468]](#cite_note-vLHxV-469) The [International Labour Organization](/wiki/International_Labour_Organization "International Labour Organization") (ILO) reported that the income generated in the first nine months of 2020 from work across the world dropped by 10.7%, or $3.5 trillion.[[469]](#cite_note-rd8bm-470)

#### Supply shortages

Main article: [Shortages related to the COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/Shortages_related_to_the_COVID-19_pandemic "Shortages related to the COVID-19 pandemic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Ntuc_super_store%2C_Singapore_%2849505410793%29.jpg/250px-Ntuc_super_store%2C_Singapore_%2849505410793%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ntuc_super_store,_Singapore_(49505410793).jpg)

COVID-19 fears led to panic buying of essentials across the world, including toilet paper, instant noodles, bread, rice, vegetables, disinfectant, and rubbing alcohol (picture taken in February 2020).

Pandemic fears led to [panic buying](/wiki/Panic_buying "Panic buying"), emptying groceries of essentials such as food, toilet paper, and bottled water. Panic buying stemmed from perceived threat, perceived scarcity, fear of the unknown, coping behaviour and social psychological factors (e.g. [social influence](/wiki/Social_influence "Social influence") and trust).[[470]](#cite_note-cSvA4-471)

Supply [shortages](/wiki/Shortage "Shortage") were due to disruption to factory and logistic operations; shortages were worsened by [supply chain](/wiki/Supply_chain "Supply chain") disruptions from factory and port shutdowns, and labour shortages.[[471]](#cite_note-XvcU9-472)

Shortages continued as managers underestimated the speed of economic recovery after the initial economic crash. The technology industry, in particular, warned of delays from underestimates of [semiconductor](/wiki/Semiconductor "Semiconductor") demand for vehicles and other products.[[472]](#cite_note-AutoDW-263-473)

According to WHO Secretary-General Tedros Ghebreyesus, demand for [personal protective equipment](/wiki/Personal_protective_equipment "Personal protective equipment") (PPE) rose one hundredfold, pushing prices up twentyfold.[[473]](#cite_note-AutoDW-275-474)[[474]](#cite_note-AutoDW-276-475) PPE stocks were exhausted everywhere.[[475]](#cite_note-Mycfo-476)[[476]](#cite_note-477)[[477]](#cite_note-478)

In September 2021, the [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank") reported that food prices remained generally stable and the supply outlook remained positive. However, the poorest countries witnessed a sharp increase in food prices, reaching the highest level since the pandemic began.[[478]](#cite_note-479)[[479]](#cite_note-auto4-480) The Agricultural Commodity Price Index stabilised in the third quarter but remained 17% higher than in January 2021.[[480]](#cite_note-481)[[479]](#cite_note-auto4-480)

By contrast, petroleum products were in surplus at the beginning of the pandemic, as demand for gasoline and other products collapsed due to reduced commuting and other trips.[[481]](#cite_note-BBC,_US_oil_prices,_21_April_2020-482) The [2021 global energy crisis](/wiki/2021_global_energy_crisis "2021 global energy crisis") was driven by a global surge in demand as the world economy recovered. Energy demand was particularly strong in Asia.[[482]](#cite_note-483)[[483]](#cite_note-484)

### Arts and cultural heritage

Main article: [Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on the arts and cultural heritage](/wiki/Impact_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic_on_the_arts_and_cultural_heritage "Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on the arts and cultural heritage")

The performing arts and [cultural heritage sectors](/wiki/Cultural_heritage_sector "Cultural heritage sector") were profoundly affected by the pandemic. Both organisations' and individuals' operations have been impacted globally. By March 2020, across the world and to varying degrees, museums, libraries, performance venues, and other cultural institutions had been indefinitely closed with their exhibitions, events and performances cancelled or postponed.[[484]](#cite_note-v5Qlx-485) A 2021 [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") report estimated ten million job losses worldwide in the culture and creative industries.[[485]](#cite_note-Ottone-486)[[486]](#cite_note-487) Some services continued through digital platforms,[[487]](#cite_note-ZljIY-488)[[488]](#cite_note-Burke2020-489)[[489]](#cite_note-SxIEK-490) such as live streaming concerts[[490]](#cite_note-SZ5Mc-491) or web-based arts festivals.[[491]](#cite_note-mYlLn-492)

### Politics

Main article: [Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on politics](/wiki/Impact_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic_on_politics "Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on politics")

See also: [Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on international relations](/wiki/Impact_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic_on_international_relations "Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on international relations")

The pandemic affected political systems, causing suspensions of legislative activities,[[492]](#cite_note-20200313nationalpost-493) isolations or deaths of politicians,[[493]](#cite_note-20200318foreignpolicy-494) and rescheduled elections.[[494]](#cite_note-20200427nytimes-495) Although they developed broad support among epidemiologists, NPIs (non-pharmaceutical interventions) were controversial in many countries. Intellectual opposition came primarily from other fields, along with heterodox epidemiologists.[[495]](#cite_note-74OHE-496)

#### Brazil

The pandemic (and the [response of Brazilian politicians](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Brazil#Responses "COVID-19 pandemic in Brazil") to it) led to widespread panic, confusion, and pessimism in Brazil.[[496]](#cite_note-BenderdaSilvaBaum2022-497) When questioned regarding record deaths in the country in April 2020, Brazilian president [Jair Bolsonaro](/wiki/Jair_Bolsonaro "Jair Bolsonaro") said "So what? I'm sorry. What do you want me to do about it?"[[497]](#cite_note-Conde_2020-498) Bolsonaro disregarded WHO-recommended mitigation techniques and instead [downplayed the risks of the virus](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Brazil#Comments_by_Bolsonaro "COVID-19 pandemic in Brazil"), promoted increased economic activity, spread [misinformation](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Brazil#Presidential_responses "COVID-19 pandemic in Brazil") about the efficacy of masks, vaccines and public health measures, and distributed unproven treatments including [hydroxychloroquine](/wiki/Chloroquine_and_hydroxychloroquine_during_the_COVID-19_pandemic "Chloroquine and hydroxychloroquine during the COVID-19 pandemic") and [ivermectin](/wiki/Ivermectin_during_the_COVID-19_pandemic "Ivermectin during the COVID-19 pandemic").[[496]](#cite_note-BenderdaSilvaBaum2022-497) A series of [federal health ministers](/wiki/List_of_Ministers_of_Health_of_Brazil "List of Ministers of Health of Brazil") resigned or were dismissed after they refused to implement Bolsonaro's policies.[[498]](#cite_note-Londono2020-499)

Disagreements between federal and state governments led to a chaotic and delayed response to the rapid spread of the virus,[[499]](#cite_note-Dantas2020-500) exacerbated by preexisting social and economic disparities in the country.[[496]](#cite_note-BenderdaSilvaBaum2022-497)[[500]](#cite_note-CarnutMendesGuerra2020-501) Employment, investment and valuation of the [Brazilian real](/wiki/Brazilian_real "Brazilian real") plummeted to record lows.[[496]](#cite_note-BenderdaSilvaBaum2022-497)[[501]](#cite_note-ReutersBrazil2020-502) Brazil was also heavily affected by the Delta and Omicron variants.[[502]](#cite_note-AlcantaraNogueiraShuabTosta2022-503) At the height of the outbreak in the spring of 2021, 3,000+ Brazilians were dying per day.[[503]](#cite_note-BBCBrazilMarch2021-504)[[504]](#cite_note-NPRBrazilApril2021-505) Bolsonaro's loss to [Lula da Silva](/wiki/Lula_da_Silva "Lula da Silva") in the [2022 presidential election](/wiki/2022_Brazilian_general_election "2022 Brazilian general election") is widely credited to the former's [mishandling of the pandemic](/wiki/Presidency_of_Jair_Bolsonaro#Response_to_COVID-19_pandemic "Presidency of Jair Bolsonaro").[[505]](#cite_note-ChomskyPolychroniou2023-506)[[506]](#cite_note-EconomistBolsonaroLoss2022-507)[[507]](#cite_note-Milhorance2022October-508)

#### China

Further information: [China–United States relations § COVID-19](/wiki/China%E2%80%93United_States_relations#COVID-19 "China–United States relations")

Multiple provincial-level administrators of the [Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party "Chinese Communist Party") (CCP) were dismissed over their handling of quarantine measures. Some commentators claimed this move was intended to protect CCP General Secretary [Xi Jinping](/wiki/Xi_Jinping "Xi Jinping").[[508]](#cite_note-vLpPa-509) The [US intelligence community](/wiki/US_intelligence_community "US intelligence community") claimed that China intentionally under-reported its COVID-19 caseload.[[509]](#cite_note-20200402nytimes-510) The Chinese government maintained that it acted swiftly and transparently.[[510]](#cite_note-hMc3w-511)[[511]](#cite_note-512) Journalists and activists in China who reported on the pandemic were detained by authorities,[[512]](#cite_note-513)[[513]](#cite_note-514) including [Zhang Zhan](/wiki/Zhang_Zhan "Zhang Zhan"), who was arrested and tortured.[[514]](#cite_note-515)[[515]](#cite_note-516)

#### Italy

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Palazzo_Margherita_lit_in_Italian_flag_to_show_solidarity_under_COVID-19_pandemic.jpg/250px-Palazzo_Margherita_lit_in_Italian_flag_to_show_solidarity_under_COVID-19_pandemic.jpg)](/wiki/File:Palazzo_Margherita_lit_in_Italian_flag_to_show_solidarity_under_COVID-19_pandemic.jpg)

Palazzo Margherita lit in the Italian flag colours to show solidarity during the COVID-19 pandemic on 26 March 2020

In early March 2020, the Italian government criticised the EU's lack of solidarity with Italy.[[516]](#cite_note-517)[[517]](#cite_note-518)[[518]](#cite_note-politico-corona-519) On 22 March 2020, after a phone call with Italian Prime Minister [Giuseppe Conte](/wiki/Giuseppe_Conte "Giuseppe Conte"), Russian president [Vladimir Putin](/wiki/Vladimir_Putin "Vladimir Putin") ordered the [Russian army](/wiki/Russian_Armed_Forces "Russian Armed Forces") to send military medics, disinfection vehicles, and other medical equipment to Italy.[[519]](#cite_note-reuters219081-520)[[520]](#cite_note-20200328insideover-521) In early April, Norway and EU states like Romania and Austria started to offer help by sending medical personnel and disinfectant,[[521]](#cite_note-eumedicalteams-522) and [European Commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission") president [Ursula von der Leyen](/wiki/Ursula_von_der_Leyen "Ursula von der Leyen") offered an official apology to the country.[[522]](#cite_note-euapology-523)

#### United States

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Open_Ohio_Rally_IMG_0910_%2849799974031%29.jpg/250px-Open_Ohio_Rally_IMG_0910_%2849799974031%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Open_Ohio_Rally_IMG_0910_(49799974031).jpg)

Anti-lockdown [protesters](/wiki/Protests_over_responses_to_the_COVID-19_pandemic "Protests over responses to the COVID-19 pandemic") rallied at the [Ohio Statehouse](/wiki/Ohio_Statehouse "Ohio Statehouse") 20 April 2020.[[523]](#cite_note-Columbus_Dispatch-524)

Beginning in mid-April 2020, protestors objected to government-imposed business closures and restrictions on personal movement and assembly.[[524]](#cite_note-5YqAF-525) Simultaneously, [essential workers](/wiki/Essential_workers "Essential workers") protested unsafe conditions and low wages by participating in a brief [general strike](/wiki/General_strike "General strike").[[525]](#cite_note-c114M-526) Some political analysts claimed that the pandemic contributed to US president [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump")'s [2020](/wiki/2020_United_States_presidential_election "2020 United States presidential election") defeat.[[526]](#cite_note-TrumpChances-527)[[527]](#cite_note-AtlanticChances-528)

The [COVID-19 pandemic in the United States](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_States "COVID-19 pandemic in the United States") prompted calls for the United States to adopt social policies common in other wealthy countries, including [universal health care](/wiki/Universal_health_care "Universal health care"), [universal child care](/w/index.php?title=Universal_child_care&action=edit&redlink=1 "Universal child care (page does not exist)"), [paid sick leave](/wiki/Paid_sick_leave "Paid sick leave"), and higher levels of funding for public health.[[528]](#cite_note-NYT_Calls-529)[[529]](#cite_note-Hill5-530)[[530]](#cite_note-Ddn2P-531) The [Kaiser Family Foundation](/wiki/Kaiser_Family_Foundation "Kaiser Family Foundation") estimated that preventable hospitalisations of unvaccinated Americans in the second half of 2021 cost US$13.8 billion.[[531]](#cite_note-Amin-532)

There were also protest in regards to vaccine mandates in the United States.[[532]](#cite_note-533) In January 2022, the US Supreme Court struck down an OSHA rule that mandated vaccination or a testing regimen for all companies with greater than 100 employees.[[533]](#cite_note-534)[[534]](#cite_note-535)

#### Other countries

The number of journalists imprisoned or detained increased worldwide; some detentions were related to the pandemic.[[535]](#cite_note-536)[[536]](#cite_note-537) The planned [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") "[Defender 2020](/wiki/List_of_NATO_exercises#2020 "List of NATO exercises")" military exercise in Germany, [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland") and the [Baltic states](/wiki/Baltic_states "Baltic states"), the largest NATO war exercise since the end of the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War"), was held on a reduced scale.[[537]](#cite_note-20200320spectator-538)[[538]](#cite_note-20200319reuters-539)

The Iranian government was heavily affected by the virus, which infected some two dozen parliament members and political figures.[[303]](#cite_note-D2Xpc-304)[[539]](#cite_note-540) Iranian president [Hassan Rouhani](/wiki/Hassan_Rouhani "Hassan Rouhani") wrote a public letter to world leaders asking for help on 14 March 2020, due to a lack of access to international markets.[[540]](#cite_note-u2rRX-541) Saudi Arabia, which had launched a [military intervention in Yemen](/wiki/Saudi_Arabian-led_intervention_in_Yemen "Saudi Arabian-led intervention in Yemen") in March 2015, declared a ceasefire.[[541]](#cite_note-20200409foreignpolicy-542)

Diplomatic relations between [Japan and South Korea](/wiki/Japan%E2%80%93South_Korea_relations "Japan–South Korea relations") worsened.[[542]](#cite_note-543) South Korea criticised Japan's "ambiguous and passive quarantine efforts" after Japan announced travellers from South Korea must quarantine for two weeks.[[543]](#cite_note-HnqN4-544) South Korean society was initially polarised on president [Moon Jae-in](/wiki/Moon_Jae-in "Moon Jae-in")'s response to the crisis; many Koreans signed petitions calling for Moon's [impeachment](/wiki/Impeachment "Impeachment") or praising his response.[[312]](#cite_note-imx38-313)

Some countries passed emergency legislation. Some commentators expressed concern that it could allow governments to strengthen their grip on power.[[544]](#cite_note-20200331theguardian-545)[[545]](#cite_note-20200330nytimes-546) In Hungary, the parliament voted to allow Prime Minister [Viktor Orbán](/wiki/Viktor_Orb%C3%A1n "Viktor Orbán") to rule by decree indefinitely, suspend parliament and elections, and punish those deemed to have spread false information.[[546]](#cite_note-20200330cnn-547) In countries such as [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt"),[[547]](#cite_note-dw53009293-548) [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey"),[[548]](#cite_note-20200401foreignpolicy-549) and [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand"),[[549]](#cite_note-550) opposition activists and government critics were [arrested](/wiki/Misinformation_related_to_the_COVID-19_pandemic#Efforts_to_combat_misinformation "Misinformation related to the COVID-19 pandemic") for allegedly spreading [fake news](/wiki/Fake_news "Fake news").[[550]](#cite_note-20200410straitstimes-551) In [India](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_India "COVID-19 pandemic in India"), journalists criticising the government's response were arrested or issued warnings by police and authorities.[[551]](#cite_note-552)

### Food systems

Further information: [Food security during the COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/Food_security_during_the_COVID-19_pandemic "Food security during the COVID-19 pandemic")

The pandemic disrupted food systems worldwide,[[552]](#cite_note-:9b-553)[[553]](#cite_note-554) hitting at a time when hunger and undernourishment were rising- an estimated 690 million people lacked food security in 2019.[[554]](#cite_note-:10-555) Food access fell – driven by falling incomes, lost remittances, and disruptions to food production.[[555]](#cite_note-556) In some cases, food prices rose.[[552]](#cite_note-:9b-553)[[554]](#cite_note-:10-555) The pandemic and its accompanying lockdowns and travel restrictions slowed movement of food aid. According to the WHO, 811 million people were undernourished in 2020, "likely related to the fallout of COVID-19".[[556]](#cite_note-557)[[435]](#cite_note-Effect_of_COVID-19_on_food_security-436)

### Education

Main article: [Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on education](/wiki/Impact_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic_on_education "Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on education")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/%D8%A7%D9%85%D8%AA%D8%AD%D8%A7%D9%86%D8%A7%D8%AA_%D9%86%D9%87%D8%A7%DB%8C%DB%8C_%D8%AF%D8%B1_%D8%B4%D8%B1%D8%A7%DB%8C%D8%B7_%DA%A9%D8%B1%D9%88%D9%86%D8%A7_3470479.jpg/250px-%D8%A7%D9%85%D8%AA%D8%AD%D8%A7%D9%86%D8%A7%D8%AA_%D9%86%D9%87%D8%A7%DB%8C%DB%8C_%D8%AF%D8%B1_%D8%B4%D8%B1%D8%A7%DB%8C%D8%B7_%DA%A9%D8%B1%D9%88%D9%86%D8%A7_3470479.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D8%A7%D9%85%D8%AA%D8%AD%D8%A7%D9%86%D8%A7%D8%AA_%D9%86%D9%87%D8%A7%DB%8C%DB%8C_%D8%AF%D8%B1_%D8%B4%D8%B1%D8%A7%DB%8C%D8%B7_%DA%A9%D8%B1%D9%88%D9%86%D8%A7_3470479.jpg)

Students take end-of-year exams in [Tabriz](/wiki/Tabriz "Tabriz"), Iran, during the pandemic.

The pandemic impacted educational systems in many countries. Many governments temporarily closed educational institutions, often replaced by [online education](/wiki/Online_education "Online education"). Other countries, such as Sweden, kept their schools open. As of September 2020, approximately 1.077 billion [learners](/wiki/Learning "Learning") were affected due to school closures. School closures impacted students, teachers, and families with far-reaching economic and societal consequences.[[557]](#cite_note-558) They shed light on social and economic issues, including [student debt](/wiki/Student_debt "Student debt"), [digital learning](/wiki/Digital_learning "Digital learning"), food insecurity, and [homelessness](/wiki/Homelessness "Homelessness"), as well as access to [childcare](/wiki/Child_care "Child care"), health care, housing, internet, and [disability services](/wiki/Disability_rights_movement "Disability rights movement"). The impact was more severe for disadvantaged children.[[558]](#cite_note-559) Many countries, including Bangladesh, granted auto promotion to the public examination candidates.[[559]](#cite_note-560) The Higher Education Policy Institute reported that around 63% of students claimed worsened mental health as a result of the pandemic.[[560]](#cite_note-561)

### Health

Main articles: [Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on other health issues](/wiki/Impact_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic_on_other_health_issues "Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on other health issues") and [Mental health during the COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/Mental_health_during_the_COVID-19_pandemic "Mental health during the COVID-19 pandemic")

The pandemic impacted global health for many conditions. Hospital visits fell.[[561]](#cite_note-562) Visits for heart attack symptoms declined by 38% in the US and 40% in Spain.[[562]](#cite_note-563) The head of cardiology at the [University of Arizona](/wiki/University_of_Arizona "University of Arizona") said, "My worry is some of these people are dying at home because they're too scared to go to the hospital".[[563]](#cite_note-Stat_News,_with_serious_heart_symptoms_away,_April_23-564) People with strokes and [appendicitis](/wiki/Appendicitis "Appendicitis") were less likely to seek treatment.[[564]](#cite_note-565)[[565]](#cite_note-566)[[563]](#cite_note-Stat_News,_with_serious_heart_symptoms_away,_April_23-564) [Medical supply shortages](/wiki/Shortages_related_to_the_COVID-19_pandemic "Shortages related to the COVID-19 pandemic") impacted many people.[[566]](#cite_note-Atlantic_Shortages-567) The pandemic impacted [mental health](/wiki/Mental_health "Mental health"),[[567]](#cite_note-568)[[568]](#cite_note-Stix_2021-569) increasing [anxiety](/wiki/Anxiety "Anxiety"), depression, and [post-traumatic stress disorder](/wiki/Post-traumatic_stress_disorder "Post-traumatic stress disorder"), affecting healthcare workers, patients and quarantined individuals.[[569]](#cite_note-570)[[570]](#cite_note-Santomauro_2021-571)

In late 2022, during the first northern hemisphere autumn and winter seasons following the widespread relaxation of global public health measures, North America and Europe experienced a surge in respiratory viruses and coinfections in both adults and children.[[571]](#cite_note-572) This formed the beginning of the 2022–2023 paediatric care crisis and what some experts termed a "[tripledemic](/wiki/Tripledemic "Tripledemic")" of seasonal influenza, [respiratory syncytial virus](/wiki/Respiratory_syncytial_virus "Respiratory syncytial virus") (RSV), and SARS-CoV-2 throughout North America.[[572]](#cite_note-573)[[573]](#cite_note-574) In the United Kingdom, paediatric infections also began to spike beyond pre-pandemic levels, albeit with different illnesses, such as [Group A streptococcal infection](/wiki/Group_A_streptococcal_infection "Group A streptococcal infection") and resultant [scarlet fever](/wiki/Scarlet_fever "Scarlet fever").[[574]](#cite_note-575)[[575]](#cite_note-United_Kingdom-576) As of mid-December 2022, 19 children in the UK had died due to [Strep A](/wiki/Streptococcus_pyogenes "Streptococcus pyogenes") and the wave of infections had begun to spread into North America and Mainland Europe.[[576]](#cite_note-Strep_A_death-577)[[577]](#cite_note-Strep_Expansion-578)

The B/Yamagata lineage of [influenza B](/wiki/Influenza_B "Influenza B") might have become extinct in 2020/2021 due to COVID-19 pandemic measures.[[578]](#cite_note-Alhoufie2021-579)[[579]](#cite_note-Koutsakos2021-580) There have been no naturally occurring cases confirmed since March 2020.[[580]](#cite_note-WHOflu2023-581)[[581]](#cite_note-UMN2023-582) In 2023, the WHO concluded that protection against the Yamagata lineage was no longer necessary in the seasonal [flu vaccine](/wiki/Flu_vaccine "Flu vaccine"), reducing the number of lineages targeted by the vaccine from four to three.[[580]](#cite_note-WHOflu2023-581)[[581]](#cite_note-UMN2023-582)

Preventative measures taken during the COVID-19 pandemic may have impacted the number of [bronchiolitis](/wiki/Bronchiolitis "Bronchiolitis") cases, with observed decreases in cases during the pandemic followed by an increase when preventative measures were rolled back.[[582]](#cite_note-Sabeena-583)[[583]](#cite_note-Hogan2025-584)

### Environment

Main article: [Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on the environment](/wiki/Impact_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic_on_the_environment "Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on the environment")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Pollutant_Drops_in_wuhan_china_due_to_virus.png/500px-Pollutant_Drops_in_wuhan_china_due_to_virus.png)](/wiki/File:Pollutant_Drops_in_wuhan_china_due_to_virus.png)

Images from the [NASA Earth Observatory](/wiki/NASA_Earth_Observatory "NASA Earth Observatory") show a stark drop in pollution in [Wuhan](/wiki/Wuhan "Wuhan"), when comparing [NO2](/wiki/Nitrogen_dioxide "Nitrogen dioxide") levels in early 2019 (top) and early 2020 (bottom).[[584]](#cite_note-585)

The pandemic and the reaction to it positively affected the [environment](/wiki/Natural_environment "Natural environment") and [climate](/wiki/Climate "Climate") as a result of reduced human activity. During the "[anthropause](/wiki/Anthropause "Anthropause")", fossil fuel use decreased, resource consumption declined, and waste disposal improved, generating less pollution.[[585]](#cite_note-:9-586) [Planned air travel](/wiki/Economic_impact_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic#Transportation "Economic impact of the COVID-19 pandemic") and vehicle transportation declined. In China, [lockdowns](/wiki/COVID-19_lockdown_in_Hubei "COVID-19 lockdown in Hubei") and other measures resulted in a 26% decrease in coal consumption, and a 50% reduction in nitrogen oxides emissions.[[585]](#cite_note-:9-586)[[586]](#cite_note-587)[[587]](#cite_note-588)

In 2020, a worldwide study on mammalian wildlife responses to human presence during COVID lockdowns found complex patterns of animal behaviour. Carnivores were generally less active when humans were around, while herbivores in developed areas were more active. Among other findings, this suggested that herbivores may view humans as a shield against predators, highlighting the importance of location and human presence history in understanding wildlife responses to changes in human activity in a given area.[[588]](#cite_note-589)

A [wide variety](/wiki/List_of_animals_that_can_get_SARS-CoV-2 "List of animals that can get SARS-CoV-2") of largely mammalian species, both captive and wild, have been shown to be susceptible to SARS-CoV-2, with some encountering a particularly high degree of fatal outcomes.[[589]](#cite_note-590) In particular, both [farmed and wild mink](/wiki/SARS-CoV-2_in_mink "SARS-CoV-2 in mink") have developed highly symptomatic and severe COVID-19 infections, with a mortality rate as high as 35–55% according to one study.[[590]](#cite_note-Frontiers_Spread-591)[[591]](#cite_note-592) [White-tailed deer](/wiki/SARS-CoV-2_in_white-tailed_deer "SARS-CoV-2 in white-tailed deer"), on the other hand, have largely avoided severe outcomes but have effectively become [natural reservoirs](/wiki/Natural_reservoir "Natural reservoir") of the virus, with large numbers of free-ranging deer infected throughout the US and Canada, including approximately 80% of [Iowa](/wiki/Iowa "Iowa")'s wild deer herd.[[592]](#cite_note-593)[[593]](#cite_note-Nature_April-594) An August 2023 study appeared to confirm the status of white-tailed deer as a disease reservoir, noting that the viral evolution of SARS-CoV-2 in deer occurs at triple the rate of its evolution in humans and that infection rates remained high, even in areas rarely frequented by humans.[[594]](#cite_note-Triple_Evolution-595)

### Discrimination and prejudice

Main article: [Xenophobia and racism related to the COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/Xenophobia_and_racism_related_to_the_COVID-19_pandemic "Xenophobia and racism related to the COVID-19 pandemic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Memorial_Day_2020_-_San_Francisco_Under_Quarantine_%2849935630543%29.jpg/250px-Memorial_Day_2020_-_San_Francisco_Under_Quarantine_%2849935630543%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Memorial_Day_2020_-_San_Francisco_Under_Quarantine_(49935630543).jpg)

A socially distanced homeless encampment in San Francisco, California, in May 2020[[595]](#cite_note-596)

Heightened prejudice, [xenophobia](/wiki/Xenophobia "Xenophobia"), and racism toward people of [Chinese and East Asian descent](/wiki/Sinophobe "Sinophobe") were documented around the world.[[596]](#cite_note-AutoDW-251-597)[[597]](#cite_note-zogcf-598) Reports from February 2020, when most confirmed cases were confined to China, cited racist sentiments about Chinese people 'deserving' the virus.[[598]](#cite_note-bangkokpost1854094-599)[[599]](#cite_note-psychologytoday202002-600)[[600]](#cite_note-20200201telegraph-601) Individuals of Asian descent in Europe and North America reported increasing instances of racially motivated abuse and assaults as a result of the pandemic.[[601]](#cite_note-NYT_Racism-602)[[602]](#cite_note-05O7n-603)[[603]](#cite_note-NYT_April_Racism-604) US president [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") was criticised for referring to SARS-CoV-2 as the "Chinese Virus" and "Kung Flu", terms which were condemned as being racist and xenophobic.[[604]](#cite_note-trumpnyt-605)[[605]](#cite_note-20200320BI-606)[[606]](#cite_note-MNCw4-607)

[Age-based discrimination](/wiki/Ageism "Ageism") against older adults increased during the pandemic. This was attributed to their perceived vulnerability and subsequent physical and social isolation measures, which, coupled with their reduced social activity, increased dependency on others. Similarly, limited digital literacy left the elderly more vulnerable to isolation, depression, and loneliness.[[607]](#cite_note-608)

In a correspondence published in *[The Lancet](/wiki/The_Lancet "The Lancet")* in 2021, German epidemiologist Günter Kampf described the harmful effects of "inappropriate stigmatisation of unvaccinated people, who include our patients, colleagues, and other fellow citizens", noting the evidence that vaccinated individuals play a large role in transmission.[[608]](#cite_note-609) American bioethicist Arthur Caplan responded to Kampf, writing "Criticising [the unvaccinated] who... wind up in hospitals and morgues in huge numbers, put stress on finite resources, and prolong the pandemic... is not stigmatising, it is deserved moral condemnation".[[609]](#cite_note-610)

In January 2022, [Amnesty International](/wiki/Amnesty_International "Amnesty International") urged [Italy](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Italy "COVID-19 pandemic in Italy") to change their anti-COVID-19 restrictions to avoid discrimination against unvaccinated people, saying that "the government must continue to ensure that the entire population can enjoy its fundamental rights". The restrictions included mandatory vaccination over the age of 50, and mandatory vaccination to use public transport.[[610]](#cite_note-611)

### Lifestyle changes

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Wee_Annie%2C_Kempock_Street%2C_face_mask.jpg/250px-Wee_Annie%2C_Kempock_Street%2C_face_mask.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wee_Annie,_Kempock_Street,_face_mask.jpg)

The "Wee Annie" statue in [Gourock](/wiki/Gourock,_Scotland "Gourock, Scotland"), Scotland, was given a face mask during the pandemic.

The pandemic triggered massive changes in behaviour, from increased Internet commerce to cultural changes in the workplace. Online retailers in the US posted $791.70 billion in sales in 2020, an increase of 32.4% from $598.02 billion the year before.[[611]](#cite_note-DDCV1ECMR-612) Home delivery orders increased, while indoor restaurant dining shut down due to lockdown orders or low sales.[[612]](#cite_note-Fo18zrty783-613)[[613]](#cite_note-Fo18zrty784-614) Hackers, [cybercriminals](/wiki/Cybercriminal "Cybercriminal") and scammers took advantage of the changes to launch new online attacks.[[614]](#cite_note-Fo18zrty787-615)

Education in some countries temporarily shifted from physical attendance to video conferencing.[[615]](#cite_note-Fo18zrty790-616) Massive layoffs shrank the airline, travel, hospitality, and other industries.[[616]](#cite_note-Fo18zrty6966-617)[[617]](#cite_note-Fo18zrty9999-618) Despite most corporations implementing measures to address COVID-19 in the workplace, a poll from [Catalyst](/wiki/Catalyst_(nonprofit_organization) "Catalyst (nonprofit organization)") found that as many as 68% of employees around the world felt that these policies were only performative and "not genuine".[[618]](#cite_note-619)

The pandemic led to a surge in [remote work](/wiki/Remote_work "Remote work"). According to a [Gallup poll](/wiki/Gallup_poll "Gallup poll"), only 4% of US employees were fully remote before the pandemic, compared to 43% in May 2020. Among white collar workers, that shift was more pronounced, with 6% increasing to 65% in the same period.[[619]](#cite_note-The_New_York_Times_2022-620) That trend continued in later stages of the pandemic, with many workers choosing to remain remote even after workplaces reopened.[[620]](#cite_note-Mitchell_2022-621)[[621]](#cite_note-Kagubare_2023-622) Many Nordic, European, and Asian companies increased their recruitment of international remote workers even as the pandemic waned, partially to save on labour costs.[[622]](#cite_note-ComputerWeekly.com_2023-623)[[623]](#cite_note-Braesemann_Stephany_Teutloff_Kässi_2022_p=e0274630-624) This also led to a talent drain in the global south and in remote areas in the global north.[[623]](#cite_note-Braesemann_Stephany_Teutloff_Kässi_2022_p=e0274630-624)[[624]](#cite_note-EuropeanStingWEF2023-625) High cost of living and dense urban areas also lost office real estate value due to remote worker exodus.[[625]](#cite_note-Economist2022Jan-626) By May 2023, due to increasing layoffs and concerns over productivity, some white collar workplaces in the US had resorted to performance review penalties and indirect incentives (e.g. donations to charity) to encourage workers to return to the office.[[626]](#cite_note-Peck_2023-627)

### Historiography

A 2021 study noted that the COVID-19 pandemic had increased interest in epidemics and infectious diseases among both historians and the general public. Prior to the pandemic, these topics were usually overlooked by "general" history and only received attention in the [history of medicine](/wiki/History_of_medicine "History of medicine").[[627]](#cite_note-628) Many comparisons were made between the COVID-19 and [1918 influenza pandemics](/wiki/1918_influenza_pandemic "1918 influenza pandemic"),[[628]](#cite_note-629)[[629]](#cite_note-630) including the development of anti-mask movements,[[630]](#cite_note-631)[[631]](#cite_note-632) the widespread promotion of misinformation[[632]](#cite_note-633)[[633]](#cite_note-634) and the impact of [socioeconomic disparities](/wiki/Social_determinants_of_health "Social determinants of health").[[634]](#cite_note-635)

### Religion

Main article: [Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on religion](/wiki/Impact_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic_on_religion "Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on religion")

[![Two men in masks, wearing mitres and red vestments, stand in front of an altar. Altar servers, deacons, and priests in the background similarly wear masks.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Solenidade_de_Pentecostes_2020%2C_m%C3%A1scaras.png/250px-Solenidade_de_Pentecostes_2020%2C_m%C3%A1scaras.png)](/wiki/File:Solenidade_de_Pentecostes_2020,_m%C3%A1scaras.png)

[Jorge Ortiga](/wiki/Jorge_Ortiga "Jorge Ortiga"), [Archbishop of Braga](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Braga "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Braga"), Portugal, wearing a protective mask during [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost") Mass in May 2020

In some areas, religious groups exacerbated the spread of the virus, through large gatherings and the dissemination of misinformation.[[635]](#cite_note-636)[[636]](#cite_note-637)[[637]](#cite_note-638) Some religious leaders decried what they saw as violations of religious freedom.[[638]](#cite_note-639) In other cases, religious identity was a beneficial factor for health, increasing compliance with public health measures and protecting against the negative effects of isolation on mental wellbeing.[[639]](#cite_note-640)[[640]](#cite_note-641)[[641]](#cite_note-642)

## Information dissemination

Further information: [Media coverage of the COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/Media_coverage_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic "Media coverage of the COVID-19 pandemic"), [Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on social media](/wiki/Impact_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic_on_social_media "Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on social media"), and [Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on journalism](/wiki/Impact_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic_on_journalism "Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on journalism")

Some news organisations removed their online paywalls for some or all of their pandemic-related articles and posts.[[642]](#cite_note-643) Many scientific publishers provided pandemic-related journal articles to the public free of charge as part of the National Institutes of Health's COVID-19 Public Health Emergency Initiative.[[643]](#cite_note-644)[[644]](#cite_note-645) According to one estimate from researchers at the University of Rome, 89.5% of COVID-19-related papers were open access, compared to an average of 48.8% for the ten most deadly human diseases.[[645]](#cite_note-646) The share of papers published on preprint servers prior to peer review increased dramatically.[[646]](#cite_note-647)

During the pandemic, [Web GIS](/wiki/Web_GIS "Web GIS") technology was leveraged to provide up to date visualisations of data related to the pandemic with the public.[[647]](#cite_note-Juergens2020-648) Employing this technology, the [Johns Hopkins University](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_University "Johns Hopkins University") COVID-19 [dashboard](/wiki/Dashboard_(computing) "Dashboard (computing)") served as the first global visualisation of COVID-19 data, which established it as the default method for government agencies to dissemeniate relevant spatial information.[[648]](#cite_note-Everts2020-649)[[649]](#cite_note-Adams2025-650) These dashboards were described by Jonathan Everts as "the most striking cultural artefact of the current coronavirus (SARS-CoV-2) pandemic",[[648]](#cite_note-Everts2020-649) and during the pandemic every state government in the United States maintained one.[[650]](#cite_note-Adams2023-651)

### Misinformation

Main article: [COVID-19 misinformation](/wiki/COVID-19_misinformation "COVID-19 misinformation")

[Misinformation](/wiki/Misinformation "Misinformation") and [conspiracy theories](/wiki/Conspiracy_theory "Conspiracy theory") about the pandemic have been widespread; they travel through [mass media](/wiki/Media_coverage_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic "Media coverage of the COVID-19 pandemic"), [social media](/wiki/Impact_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic_on_social_media#Misinformation "Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on social media") and text messaging.[[651]](#cite_note-FTTextMsg-652) In March 2020, WHO declared an "[infodemic](/wiki/Infodemic "Infodemic")" of incorrect information.[[652]](#cite_note-Lowy-653) [Cognitive biases](/wiki/Cognitive_bias "Cognitive bias"), such as [confirmation bias](/wiki/Confirmation_bias "Confirmation bias"), are linked to conspiracy beliefs, including [COVID-19 vaccine hesitancy](/wiki/COVID-19_vaccine_hesitancy "COVID-19 vaccine hesitancy").[[653]](#cite_note-654)

## Culture and society

Further information: [COVID-19 pandemic in popular culture](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_popular_culture "COVID-19 pandemic in popular culture")

The COVID-19 pandemic had a major impact on popular culture.
