It was included in the narratives of ongoing pre-pandemic television series and become a central narrative in new ones, with mixed results.[[654]](#cite_note-655) Writing for *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* about the then-upcoming [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") sitcom *[Pandemonium](/wiki/Here_We_Go_(TV_series)#Pilot "Here We Go (TV series)")* on 16 December 2020, [David Segal](/wiki/David_Segal_(journalist) "David Segal (journalist)") asked, "Are we ready to laugh about Covid-19? Or rather, is there anything amusing, or recognizable in a humorous way, about life during a plague, with all of its indignities and setbacks, not to mention its rituals and rules".[[655]](#cite_note-656)

The pandemic had driven some people to seek peaceful [escapism](/wiki/Escapism "Escapism") in media, while others were drawn towards fictional pandemics (e.g. [zombie apocalypses](/wiki/Zombie_apocalypse "Zombie apocalypse")) as an alternate form of escapism.[[656]](#cite_note-657) Common themes have included [contagion](/wiki/Contagious_disease "Contagious disease"), [isolation](/wiki/Isolation_(health_care) "Isolation (health care)") and loss of [control](/wiki/Control_(psychology) "Control (psychology)").[[657]](#cite_note-658) Many drew comparisons to the fictional film *[Contagion](/wiki/Contagion_(2011_film) "Contagion (2011 film)")* (2011),[[658]](#cite_note-659)[[659]](#cite_note-660) praising its accuracies while noting some differences,[[660]](#cite_note-661) such as the lack of an orderly vaccine rollout.[[661]](#cite_note-662)[[662]](#cite_note-663)

As people turned to music to relieve emotions evoked by the pandemic, [Spotify](/wiki/Spotify "Spotify") listenership showed that classical, [ambient](/wiki/Ambient_music "Ambient music") and [children's](/wiki/Children%27s_music "Children's music") genres grew, while pop, [country](/wiki/Country_music "Country music") and dance remained relatively stable.[[663]](#cite_note-664)

## Transition to later phases

Main article: [Endemic COVID-19](/wiki/Endemic_COVID-19 "Endemic COVID-19")

A March 2022 review declared a transition to endemic status to be "inevitable".[[664]](#cite_note-665) In June 2022, an article in *[Human Genomics](/wiki/Human_Genome_Organisation "Human Genome Organisation")* said that the pandemic was still "raging", but that "now is the time to explore the transition from the pandemic to the endemic phase".[[665]](#cite_note-666) Another review that month predicted that the virus that causes COVID-19 would become the fifth endemic seasonal coronavirus, alongside four other [human coronaviruses](/wiki/HCoV "HCoV").[[666]](#cite_note-667) A February 2023 review of the four [common cold](/wiki/Common_cold "Common cold") coronaviruses concluded that the virus would become seasonal and, like the common cold, cause less severe disease for most people.[[667]](#cite_note-668) Another 2023 review stated that the transition to endemic COVID-19 may take years or decades.[[668]](#cite_note-669)

On 5 May 2023, the WHO declared that the pandemic was no longer a [public health emergency of international concern](/wiki/Public_health_emergency_of_international_concern "Public health emergency of international concern").[[669]](#cite_note-670) This led several media outlets to incorrectly report that this meant the pandemic was "over". The WHO commented to [Full Fact](/wiki/Full_Fact "Full Fact") that it was unlikely to declare the pandemic over "in the near future" and mentioned [cholera](/wiki/Cholera "Cholera"), which it considers to have been a [pandemic since 1961](/wiki/Seventh_cholera_pandemic "Seventh cholera pandemic") (i.e., continuously for the last 64 years).[[670]](#cite_note-ff-671) The WHO does not have an official category for pandemics or make declarations of when pandemics start or end.[[4]](#cite_note-reuters-4)[[260]](#cite_note-guardian-261)[[671]](#cite_note-672)[[15]](#cite_note-timemarch2024-15) In June 2023, [Hans Kluge](/wiki/Hans_Kluge "Hans Kluge"), director of the WHO in Europe, commented that "While the international public health emergency may have ended, the pandemic certainly has not".[[672]](#cite_note-kluge-673)

Epidemics and pandemics usually end when the disease becomes [endemic](/wiki/Endemic_COVID-19 "Endemic COVID-19"), and when the disease becomes "an accepted, manageable part of normal life in a given society".[[16]](#cite_note-charters2021-16) As of March 2024, there was no widely agreed definition of when a disease is or is not a pandemic, though efforts at a formal definition were underway. Experts asked by *[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")* that month noted that COVID-19 continued to circulate and cause disease, but expressed uncertainty as to whether it should still be described as a pandemic.[[15]](#cite_note-timemarch2024-15) In December 2024, [Director-General of the World Health Organization](/wiki/Director-General_of_the_World_Health_Organization "Director-General of the World Health Organization") [Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus](/wiki/Tedros_Adhanom_Ghebreyesus "Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus") stated that COVID should not be referred to in past tense as it is "still with us, still causes acute disease and [Long COVID](/wiki/Long_COVID "Long COVID") and still kills... The world might want to forget about COVID-19, but we cannot afford to."[[673]](#cite_note-pasttense-who-674)

By 2025, five years after the start of the pandemic, experts asked by *[The Independent](/wiki/The_Independent "The Independent")* and *[The Washington Post](/wiki/The_Washington_Post "The Washington Post")* generally considered COVID-19 to have become endemic.[[13]](#cite_note-:0-13)[[14]](#cite_note-:3-14) Cases and deaths from COVID-19 still remained high; in the United States, the only two infectious diseases causing more than 10,000 deaths a year were flu and COVID, according to an expert in *The Washington Post*.[[13]](#cite_note-:0-13)

## Long-term effects

### Economic

Despite strong economic rebounds following the initial lockdowns in early 2020, towards the latter phases of the pandemic, many countries began to experience long-term economic effects. Several countries saw high [inflation rates](/wiki/Inflation_rate "Inflation rate") which had global impacts, particularly in developing countries.[[674]](#cite_note-675) Some economic impacts such as [supply chain](/wiki/Supply_chain "Supply chain") and trade operations were seen as more permanent as the pandemic exposed major weaknesses in these systems.[[675]](#cite_note-676)

In Australia, the pandemic caused an increase in [occupational burnout](/wiki/Occupational_burnout "Occupational burnout") in 2022.[[676]](#cite_note-677)

During the pandemic, a large percentage of workers in Canada came to prefer working from home, which had an impact on the traditional work model. Some corporations made efforts to force workers to return to work on-site, while some embraced the idea.[[677]](#cite_note-678)

### Travel

There was a "travel boom" causing air travel to recover at rates faster than anticipated, and the aviation industry became profitable in 2023 for the first time since 2019, before the pandemic.[[678]](#cite_note-679) However, economic issues meant some predicted that the boom would begin to slow down.[[679]](#cite_note-680) Business travel on airlines was still below pre-pandemic levels and is predicted not to recover.[[680]](#cite_note-681)

### Health

An increase in excess deaths from underlying causes not related to COVID-19 has been largely blamed on systematic issues causing delays in health care and screening during the pandemic, which has resulted in an increase of non-COVID-19 related deaths.[[681]](#cite_note-682)

### Immunisations

During the pandemic, millions of children missed out on vaccinations as countries focused efforts on combating COVID-19. Efforts were made to increase vaccination rates among children in [low-income countries](/wiki/Low-income_countries "Low-income countries"). These efforts were successful in increasing vaccination rates for some diseases, though the UN noted that post-pandemic [measles vaccinations](/wiki/Measles_vaccination "Measles vaccination") were still falling behind.[[682]](#cite_note-683)

Some of the decrease in immunisation was driven by an increase in mistrust of public health officials. This was seen in both low-income and high-income countries. Several [African countries](/wiki/African_countries "African countries") saw a decline in vaccinations due to misinformation around the pandemic flowing into other areas.[[683]](#cite_note-684) Immunisation rates have yet to recover in the United States[[684]](#cite_note-685) and the United Kingdom.[[685]](#cite_note-686)

"*The strategy of control and maximum suppression (zero-COVID) has been implemented successfully in a number of countries. The objective of this strategy is to keep transmission of the virus as close to zero as possible and ultimately to eliminate it entirely from particular geographical areas. The strategy aims to increase the capacity to identify and trace chains of transmission and to identify and manage outbreaks, while also integrating economic, psychological, social and healthcare support to guarantee the isolation of cases and contacts. This approach is also known as "Find, Test, Trace, Isolate and Support" (FTTIS)*"
